title: Scheduled Firewall Rule Review Evidence for CDE NSCs

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI DSS 1.2.8 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 1.2.8 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.1: Scheduled Firewall Rule Review Evidence for CDE NSCs.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.11.1' running on sourcetype cisco:asa and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=netfw` `sourcetype IN ("pan:config","pan:system")` — commits, admin user, rule object names; optional `sourcetype="cisco:asa"` configuration events
app: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757), Splunk Enterprise Security (263)
spl:
index=netfw sourcetype IN ("pan:config","pan:system") earliest=-185d
| where match(_raw,"(?i)commit|policy|rule|security|nat")
| lookup pci_cde_device_groups.csv device_group OUTPUT in_cde
| where in_cde="true"
| rex field=_raw "(?i)admin@?(?<fw_admin>\\S+)"
| bin _time span=30d
| stats values(fw_admin) as reviewers, dc(_raw) as change_events by _time, host
| sort - _time
description: Produces a repeatable inventory of firewall policy changes touching the cardholder data environment so assessors can see that network security controls are formally reviewed at least every six months.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for PCI DSS compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes netfw and sourcetypes cisco:asa, pan:config, pan:system. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain `pci_cde_device_groups.csv` mapping Panorama device groups or firewall hostnames to `in_cde`. (2) Align the search window to your documented six-month review cycle. (3) Export the stats table monthly and attach the export ID to the GRC ticket. (4) Exclude template pushes that do not alter enforced policy using a `commit_id` dedupe if available. Indexes required: `index=netfw`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=cisco:asa`, `sourcetype=pan:config`, `sourcetype=pan:system`.
visualization: Column chart (change_events by month), table (reviewers per device group), single value (distinct review months with activity).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.dest span=30d | sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks

knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757), Splunk Enterprise Security (263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=netfw` `sourcetype IN ("pan:config","pan:system")` — commits, admin user, rule object names; optional `sourcetype="cisco:asa"` configuration events.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain `pci_cde_device_groups.csv` mapping Panorama device groups or firewall hostnames to `in_cde`. (2) Align the search window to your documented six-month review cycle. (3) Export the stats table monthly and attach the export ID to the GRC ticket. (4) Exclude template pushes that do not alter enforced policy using a `commit_id` dedupe if available.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=netfw sourcetype IN ("pan:config","pan:system") earliest=-185d
| where match(_raw,"(?i)commit|policy|rule|security|nat")
| lookup pci_cde_device_groups.csv device_group OUTPUT in_cde
| where in_cde="true"
| rex field=_raw "(?i)admin@?(?<fw_admin>\\S+)"
| bin _time span=30d
| stats values(fw_admin) as reviewers, dc(_raw) as change_events by _time, host
| sort - _time
```

Understanding this SPL

**Scheduled Firewall Rule Review Evidence for CDE NSCs (PCI DSS Req 1.1.6, 1.2.8)** — Produces a repeatable inventory of firewall policy changes touching the cardholder data environment so assessors can see that network security controls are formally reviewed at least every six months.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=netfw` `sourcetype IN ("pan:config","pan:system")` — commits, admin user, rule object names; optional `sourcetype="cisco:asa"` configuration events. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757), Splunk Enterprise Security (263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: netfw.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=netfw, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters the current rows with `where match(_raw,"(?i)commit|policy|rule|security|nat")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where in_cde="true"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Extracts fields with `rex` (regular expression).
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time, host** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.dest span=30d | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Scheduled Firewall Rule Review Evidence for CDE NSCs (PCI DSS Req 1.1.6, 1.2.8)** — Produces a repeatable inventory of firewall policy changes touching the cardholder data environment so assessors can see that network security controls are formally reviewed at least every six months.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=netfw` `sourcetype IN ("pan:config","pan:system")` — commits, admin user, rule object names; optional `sourcetype="cisco:asa"` configuration events. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757), Splunk Enterprise Security (263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Change.All_Changes` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Column chart (change_events by month), table (reviewers per device group), single value (distinct review months with activity).
grandmaExplanation: We watch for produces a repeatable inventory of firewall policy changes touching the cardholder data environment so assessors can see that network security controls are formally reviewed at least every six months. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
